coredata.dc:title: Tunable dual photoluminescence from synthesized urea-based carbon quantum dots: A DFT based simulation on structural insights

coredata.dc:description: The current work focuses on synthesis and characterization of fluorescent Urea-based Carbon Quantum Dots (UCQDs) through a green and simplistic single-step hydrothermal treatment from citric acid and urea combination. The synthesized UCQDs have been characterized by FESEM, X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS), UV–Vis absorption, Photoluminescence, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy and Life time analysis. Concentration dependent variable particle sizes (4 nm–40 nm) were obtained for prepared UCQDs and their existence were further verified by UV–Vis absorption data and FESEM image analysis. The particle size was found to be directly proportional to the concentration of UCQDs, whereas the decrement of band gap was found to be inversely proportional to the size of UCQDs. Dual emission from UCQDs were appeared from graphite core (π → π*) at 460 nm and from passivated state (n → π*) at 530 nm. Existence of two excited state species were further verified by average lifetimes of 1.89 ns and 1.96 ns. XPS and EDX analysis confirmed the presence of three elements (C, N, O) and there bonding interactions which served as crucial inputs for the designing of potential molecular structure of synthesized UCQDs by theoretical modeling. Density functional theory (DFT) and time-dependent density functional theory (TDDFT) have been used as ab initio methods for the structural insights and spectroscopic analysis of probe UCQDs.

head.ce:title: Tunable dual photoluminescence from synthesized urea-based carbon quantum dots: A DFT based simulation on structural insights

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The current work focuses on synthesis and characterization of fluorescent Urea-based Carbon Quantum Dots (UCQDs) through a green and simplistic single-step hydrothermal treatment from citric acid and urea combination. The synthesized UCQDs have been characterized by FESEM, X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS), UV–Vis absorption, Photoluminescence, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy and Life time analysis. Concentration dependent variable particle sizes (4 nm–40 nm) were obtained for prepared UCQDs and their existence were further verified by UV–Vis absorption data and FESEM image analysis. The particle size was found to be directly proportional to the concentration of UCQDs, whereas the decrement of band gap was found to be inversely proportional to the size of UCQDs. Dual emission from UCQDs were appeared from graphite core (π → π*) at 460 nm and from passivated state (n → π*) at 530 nm. Existence of two excited state species were further verified by average lifetimes of 1.89 ns and 1.96 ns. XPS and EDX analysis confirmed the presence of three elements (C, N, O) and there bonding interactions which served as crucial inputs for the designing of potential molecular structure of synthesized UCQDs by theoretical modeling. Density functional theory (DFT) and time-dependent density functional theory (TDDFT) have been used as ab initio methods for the structural insights and spectroscopic analysis of probe UCQDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The size dependent optical and electronic properties of carbon-based nano materials induced by quantum confinement effect have attracted a wide spread global research attention [1]. In present carbon industry, available carbonic based nano sized materials are: fullerenes, carbon nanotubes, graphene sheets, nano diamonds, etc. having dimension range from 20 to 200 nm [2]. Carbon-based quantum dots which include carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are the newly invented carbonic nanomaterials having dimension less than 10 nm [3]. Generally Quantum dots are a specific class of semiconductor nanomaterials that exhibit quantum-confined whose radii are lesser than bulk exciton Bohr diameter (1–20 nm) [4]. The electronic energy levels become discretized as a result of confinement, compared to the continuous bands seen in conventional semiconductors. The band gap can be “tuned” in the quantum confined regime to produce suitable electrical and optical features, such as tuneable, symmetric narrow bandwidth emission spectra [5]. Quantum dots (QDs), with tuneable optical-based properties, have emerged in recent years as fascinating new raw materials for applications in quantum and photonics information processing [6]. However, the fundamental attractiveness of QDs is their sharp emission profile which is tuneable [7], high optical extinction coefficients [8], carrier multiplication ability [9] and variable nonlinear properties [10], etc., which can be altered by changing shape and size. Due to their many attractive properties, Quantum dots have fascinated significant attention in many applications, notably as innovative luminescent sensors [11–14], quantum computing [15], solar cell [16], nano scale display devices [17], quantum dot lasers [18], white and blue light-emitting diode (LEDs) [19], etc. However, due to the presence of heavy metal ions, all available semiconductor-based quantum dots possess high toxicity which prohibited them from any applicability related to living being. Also, presence of heavy metals makes them costly. This helpless situation encouraged the researchers to create a new materials named carbon based QDs (CQDs) to replace semiconductor based toxic quantum dots. Easy method of low cost synthesis, availability in small sizes (2 nm–10 nm), good chemical stability, dispersibility in water, good photostability, up conversion, low toxicity and tunable photoluminescence make CQDs a new category of luminous materials that outperform all other fluorescent organic dyes and luminescent inorganic quantum dots (QDs) currently available [20]. Before CQDs appeared in market, various semiconductor quantum dots have dominated the sensing and bioimaging industries for several years [11]. CQD's low toxicity, stability, favourable chemical properties and tuneable optical properties easily make them as powerful replacement among all existing fluorescent probes. From the last ten years CQDs have gradually become a rising star in the sensing, imaging, optoelectronics, LED, Solar cell, display device etc. industries due to their fascinating chemical, electronic, physiochemical, optical properties.

CQDs were invented accidently in 2004 during the purification of single walled carbon nanotubes and got the name ‘carbon quantum dots’ in 2006 [21]. The CQDs are small carbon-based nanoparticles mostly consists of graphite sp2 aromatic core surrounded by many sp3 hybridized carbon matrix as surface making their dimension from 2 to 10 nm [22]. The aromatic core of CQDs include different surface dangling bonds due to the presence of different functionalization or medication by various organic based functional groups like carbonyl (≥CO), carboxylic (≥COOH), amino (>NH2), hydroxyl (-OH) etc [23,24]. Presence of dangling bonds appear as the major reason for the non-fluorescent nature of bare CQDs [25]. Surface passivation by the introduction of excess presence of various external groups such as poly (ethylene glycol) (PEG), poly(propionylethylenimine-co-ethylenimine) (PPEI-EI), amino, hydroxyl etc, on the surface of CQDs usually enhances the fluorescence quantum yields of bare CQDs [11,21,22,26,27]. Surface passivation of CQDs usually modifies the basic physical properties of bare CQDs like increasing solubility in aqueous and non-aqueous solvents and decreasing toxicity. Moreover, in passivated state due to the abundant presence of surface functional groups, CQDs possess a strong capability to bind with many other organic, polymeric, inorganic or biological materials [22]. Strong binding with various external media may help CQDs to manipulate various controllable chemical treatments which instigate their applicability in the areas of the photocatalysis [28], optoelectronics [29], biochemical, chemical sensing [30], bioimaging [31] and drug carrier activities [32]. CQDs are used in bioimaging, biosensor, and drug administration due to their low toxicity and strong biocompatibility.

Presence of electron donors and acceptors in the individual molecular levels of CQDs afford them to show their outstanding electronic and optical properties like chemiluminescence, electrochemical luminescence, photoluminescence which bestow them with broad potentials in the area of optical sensors, optronics and catalysis [33]. However, the exact understanding about the structure of CQDs and the origin of variable optical responses, especially the existence of variable photoluminescence is not very clear till now. Available studies suggested that tuneable photoluminescence from CQDs are sometime observed as excitation dependent and sometime they show excitation independent optical characteristics [34]. Many reported studies also suggested that tuneable photoluminescence from CQDs are due to their variable sizes [24]. Till now many mechanisms have been proposed by many researchers based on particle-size distribution, charge transfer of aromatic molecules, surface traps, quantum effects, presence of intramolecular hydrogen bonds, formation, radiation recombination of excitons [3,24,35] etc to justify tuneability of photoluminescence but none of them can be considered as totally acceptable universal interpretation. Less information about the CQD structure, their variable characteristics and lack of controllable synthesis methods hinders the development and widespread applications of CQD based materials.

CQD synthesis is mainly classified by “Top-down” and “Bottom-up” approaches. These approaches include electrochemical and metal catalysed approach [36], chemical oxidation [37], combustion/calcination [38], solvothermal [39], pyrolysis [40], hydrothermal [41], laser irradiation [42], microwave assisted synthesis [43], etc. Most of the reported methods utilize chemicals for the synthesis of CQDs. Some of the mentioned methods like pyrolysis, hydrothermal, laser irradiation uses purely greener and cost-effective approach for the preparation of CQDs from various organic resources. All green carbon sources contain carbohydrates, lipids, proteins, cellulose etc. Green biological materials like vegetables, leaves, fruit peels, fruit juice, herbs, animal derivatives, bio waste can be used as carbon sources for the synthesis of green nontoxic and cost effective CQDs. Our motivation for the present work is to synthesize CQDs from some easily available organic sources by using a cost-effective, less time-consuming greener hydrothermal technique and to characterize prepared CQD's structural and multifunctional opto-chemical properties.

We have used citric acid and urea as the starting precursors for the synthesis of Urea-based Carbon Quantum Dots (UCQDs) which are found in nature in abundant quantity. Urea, also known as carbamide or diamide of carbonic acid (H2NCONH2) is a colourless organic waste product of many living organisms. It is the nitrogenous nontoxic end product which is created in liver from toxic ammonia by the metabolic breakdown of proteins in all mammals. Urea can be easily transported through blood and kidneys. It is one of the major organic components of human urine [44]. Citric acid (HOC(CH₂CO₂H)₂) is an colourless weak crystalline organic compound from the family of carboxylic acid. It is found in all plants and in many animal tissues and fluids. Citric acid naturally found in citrus fruits like lime and lemon [45]. For the present work we have used a one-step green hydrothermal process to synthesize fluorescent UCQDs from urea and citric acid combination without using any external chemicals. Synthesized UCQDs have shown excitation independent dual emission in the visible region. To understand the real reason for the dual luminescence, insights into the molecular structure and its characteristics of the synthesized UCQD in ground state (S0) and excited states (S1, S2, S3 etc.) is to be understood. We have used density functional theory (DFT) based computational approach to analyse the structural information of synthesized UCQDs as it is a very appropriate method to simulate and predict some of the crucial properties of CQDs. DFT calculations data have been compared with experimental spectroscopic data. A good agreement between computational and experimental data have helped us to analyse the structural and optoelectronic characteristics of synthesized UCQDs in terms of their applications in the fields of sensing, optoelectronics, energy devices and bio-imaging.

Citric acid (C6H8O7) with purity of 99.00 % and Urea (CH4N2O) with purity of ≥99.00 % were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Autoclave was used for hydrothermal treatment. Mili-Q water was used for solution preparation.

A PerkinElmer spectrophotometer (model Lambda-35) with a variable slit width in the range 190–900 nm and a resolution of 1 nm was used to record the UV–Vis absorption spectra at 300 K. All luminescence measurements were made with a PerkinElmer spectrophotometer (Model Fluorescence-55) ranging from 200 to 900 nm with a varying slit width with a resolution of 0.5 nm. The Model LS 55 series uses a pulsed Xenon lamp as a source of excitation. Fourier transform infrared spectra was recorded by PerkinElmer FTIR spectrophotometer ranging from 400 to 4000 cm−1 with resolution of 1 cm−1. All optical measurements were performed at room temperature under ambient condition. X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) spectrum was obtained from Model Axis Supra with Monochromatic X-ray source: Al Kα (1486.6 eV) from Kratos Analytical Ltd. The lifetime decay measurements were made by using the laser excitation source of 343 nm having pulse width (FWHM) of 260 femto second. The used exciting source wavelength of 343 nm has been created from third harmonic of 1030 nm source brought from NKT Photonics, Denmark. The fluorescence emissions were collected by f-matched lens system followed by optical fibers, before incident on SPAD. A narrow-bandpass filter (462 nm) was kept, before the SPAD to avoid the excitation wavelength falling on the detector and to avoid any damage to SPAD. High Resolution Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope (FESEM) was executed on 7610F Plus/JEOL with an energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) for image and elemental analysis with accelerating voltage 0.1 kV–30 kV.

Till today the exact structures and mechanism of fluorescence properties of CQDs are not known. Lack of knowledge about the controlled synthesis methods and very less understanding about reasons of tuneable photoluminescence properties have hindered the widespread applications of CQDs. Due to the presence of complex heterogeneity in CQD structure, many contradictory hypotheses arise from inconsistent experimental observations [46]. If the structural and physical characteristics of CQDs is to be understood then the molecular insight about the CQDs are required. So, a systematic computational and experimental investigation is required to identify the mechanism involved in variable optical signal. Specifically, we are interested on the exact mechanism of emission of CQDs in order to tuned them from deep ultraviolet to near infrared by their controlled size, shape, configuration, defects and hybridizations created by carbon networks. Theoretical data has always been proven to be useful for structural investigations of many molecular systems, with a strong connection between computational (DFT and TD-DFT) and experimental (Raman, FTIR, UV–Vis absorption, and fluorescence) results. In present study all theoretical computations, consisting of ground state (S0) and excited states (S1, S2, S3 etc.) optimization and energy calculation of probe CQDs are carried out using DFT (Density Functional Theory) [47] and TDDFT (Time Dependent Density Functional Theory) performed by Gaussian 09 [48]. We have optimized various geometries (ground state and excited states) of CQDs (A-I) with Becke's three parameter hybrid exchange function and Lee–Yang–Parr gradient corrected correlation functional (B3LYP hybrid functional) [49] by using a 6–311 G basis set together with integral equation formalism Polarizable Continuum Model (IEFPCM) for solvation effect. The optimized, stable geometries are then used as starting points for all further calculations. The optimized structures were then subjected to frequency calculations. The excited state geometries were optimized with TDDFT by using B3LYP hybrid functional with the same basis set. The vertical excitation energies for S0→S1, S2 and S3 transitions of the prove CQDs in water medium have been computed using TDDFT. To investigate the emissive properties, the guess geometry of the S0 state is used to determine the optimized geometry of a certain excited state [50]. All system computations are performed at 1 atmospheric pressure and 298.15 K temperature. The dipole transition matrix elements were used to calculate oscillator strengths and dipole moments. The resultant theoretical UV–Vis absorption, emission and IR spectra obtained from the software were compared with results obtained from experimental UV–Vis absorption, emission and FTIR to complete the chemical structure interpretation.

UCQDs were synthesized from urea and citric acid by taking them as starting precursors, we have used cost effective hydrothermal carbonization method at 160 °C for 4 h though purely in a green synthesis way. A schematic diagram of UCQDs synthesis is shown in Scheme 1 . The preliminary solution was prepared from (1:1) urea (0.1g): citric acid (0.1g) combination dissolved in fixed volume (15 ml) of Mili-Q water. The mixture then placed in a magnetic starrier for 2–4 h till colourless state of the mixture was reached. The output from magnetic starrier was then placed for sonication for 30–60 min 15 ml of sonicated solution was transferred into a 25 ml Teflon tube. Teflon tube was then placed in an autoclave for 4 h at 160 °C. Same hydrothermal procedure has been repeated for variable time period from 4 to 6 h and with variable temperature range (140 °C–170 °C). No significant change in appearance of product UCQDs (colour, concentration phase, etc) were observed after hydrothermal process. Each output solution became uniform deep brown in colour (Scheme 1). The cooled output solution from hydrothermal treatment then passed through the centrifugation process at 4000 rpm for 35 min to distinct the uniform-sized UCQDs. Time and speed are the two important parameters related to centrifugation which decide the size of QDs. Since particles of different sizes sediment at different rates, with the larger particles precipitate faster than smaller particles. Larger the time and speed of centrifugation better will be the selection of particular size of QDs [51]. The centrifuged output was filtered by 0.22 μm nylon membrane filter to get the product UCQDs in solution phase. UCQDs concentration was further diluted adding 20 μl, 100 μl and 180 μl of parent mixture in 3 ml of solvent individually denoted by [UCQDs (ml): solvent(ml)]: C1 (0.02:3), C2 (0.1:3) and C3 (0.18:3) respectively in water environment for further study. The synthesized UCQDs prepared by present method under daylight looked like yellow/light brown in colour. Depending on their sizes and presence of various surface functional groups, CQDs usually show tuneable emission profile by variable excitation [52]. In the present case, our synthesized UCQDs emits indigo colour under UV light. Since UCQDs have been synthesized from urea and citric acid, the probability of presence of amine groups (-NH2) including other functional groups like carbonyl (-C=O), carboxyl (-COOH), hydroxyl (-OH), -C-O-C-, O=C–H and other oxygen-containing groups on the surface and edge of UCQDs increases.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–Vis Absorption analysis of synthesized UCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: UV–Vis spectrum of synthesized UCQDs in water environment are shown in Fig. 1 a, b. Many parameters may affect the dimension of CQDs which include source concentration, heating time, temperature etc. For the present case, with variable time period from 4 to 6 h and with variable temperature range (140 °C–170 °C) of hydrothermal synthesis, all UCQD output in their just synthesized concentrated phase appeared as dark brown under day light and have shown saturated absorbance in far UV and VIS range having multiple absorption peaks with a weak and asymmetric shoulder in VIS region (Fig. 1a). As from concentrated phase UCQDs were diluted multiple times by adding water, the absorbance started decreasing with the appearance of very few prominent absorption peaks throughout the whole spectral range. As the dilution increases, absorbance of each peak decreases while maintaining similar absorption characteristics without changing their absorption wavelengths (Fig. 1a). UCQDs in its lowest concentration C1 looks like transparent or pale indigo under white light or under UV light with one prominent absorption peak at ∼341 nm and a broad shoulder at ∼432 nm (Fig. 1b) [53,54]. The origin of these two peaks is related to π→π* and nonbonding (n) electron transition n→π* in oxygen containing UCQDs. At concentrated phase, the UCQD surfaces used to be trapped by the presence of various surface dandling bonds (C=O, O=C–H, C–O–C) which can easily hamper the passivation of QD surfaces [25]. In concentrated non-passivated phase, the UCQD surface used to covered by several surface traps with different energy levels below LUMO [34]. Due to the presence of more energy levels, different electronic transition possibilities may arise and so multi absorption peaks may appear in concentrated phase (Fig. 1a). Being auxochromes, presence of amino groups of urea may play vital role in surface passivation around graphite core of synthesized UCQDs [34]. At lowest concentration C1 of UCQDs, amino groups can easily passivate/modify the surface of UCQDs by removing surface dangling bonds (O=C–H,C=O, C–O–C) which may result in fewer, but strong stabilized electronic transitions. As appeared in the present case, the lower wavelength sharp peak at 341 nm is due to π–π* transition in nature which arises due to the presence of conjugated C=C bonds of the core graphite structure and from the electron transition in the oxygen-containing UCQDs [55]. The appearance of blue under UV light of UCQDs also justifies the higher concentration of amino groups on UCQD surface. Amino groups are electron donors that increase the probability of electron transfer from ground to excited state (π–π*), hence raising the conjugation degree [56]. The appearance of higher wavelength broad shoulder at 432 nm is related to the n–π* transition due to the passivated state by the presence of other functional groups corresponds to the C=O bond present over the surface of UCQDs [57].

(absorption band edge, “unit less”) was converted into an associated particle size (2R) using Henglein's empirical curve which relates the wavelength of the absorption threshold to the diameter of the CQDs particles. To determine particle size the curve was digitized and the best fitting function was used as [58],

is the absorption band edge and 2R is the diameter of the UCQD. From the intersection of sharply decreasing region of the lower energy band region of the spectrum with the base line, absorption band edge can be determined. As per fitting mechanism higher the absorption band edge means higher the particle size. For concentrated UCQDs, due to agglomeration formation and non-passivation, the particle sizes were measured between 10 and 40 nm (Inset of Fig. 1a) which is further verified with the FESEM data reported in next section (Table 1 ). At lower concentration, the synthesized UCQDs were look like pale blue and the particle sizes were measured between 4 and 10 nm (Inset of Fig. 1b–Table 1) which again validated FESEM data.

To get an idea about the electronic condition of the synthesized UCQDs we have computed the energy band gap (Δε). Δε can be estimated from electronic absorption data using well known Tauc relation (2) as [59,60].
(2)
α h ν = C
(
h ν − Δ ε
)
n
where, α is an absorption coefficient calculated from Beer-Lambert᾽s law, C is constant, Δε is energy band gap of UCQDs, n is constant. The values of n = 1/2 for direct and n = 2 for indirect allowed band gap transitions [61,62]. The average Δε has been obtained from the intercept of the linear portion of (αhυ)2 versus hυ plot (Fig. 2 ) as 3.07 eV, 2.99 eV and 2.91 eV corresponding to C1, C2 and C3 concentration respectively. It was observed that as the concentration of nanoparticles increases, their size increases while the Δε decreases. As per quantum confinement rule smaller dimension of nanoparticle possess more discrete energy levels, resulting in larger Δε and vice versa (Fig. 2). The direct nature of observed band gap of UCQDs justifies the presence of radiative recombination in UCQD structure which allow the synthesized UCQDs to show effective light emissivity and possible electrochemiluminescence effectivity. Possible optical emissive efficiency justifies UCQD's candidature to be used as electroluminescent devices [63]. Stability of the synthesized UCQDs were also verified by their unaffected absorbance (shape, size) and peak positions over the period of two months which nullifies the possibility of any agglomeration and bulk phase formation for the synthesized UCQDs in present case (Supporting document (SD1)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: FESEM image analysis of synthesized UCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Size and surface morphology of synthesized UCQDs were investigated using FESEM images in their lower and higher concentrated phases. FESEM image of UCQDs at their lower concentration shows that particles are loosely packed, regular and spherical in shape (Fig. 3 a). The average particle size obtained through the histogram analysis is 6.7 nm i.e. less than 10 nm (Table 1). In concentrated phase of UCQDs, uniformity of the structure disappeared which validate the presence of agglomeration in the concentrated phase of UCQDs (Fig. 3 b). In concentrated phase the average particle sizes were obtained between 10 and 40 nm due the existence of irregular in shape with size in this phase (SD2).

In order to get an idea about the present elements in synthesized UCQDs, EDX data have been investigated (Fig. 3 c). EDS spectrum of synthesized UCQD confirms the existence of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen in terms of their weight percentages of energy peaks. The weight percentage of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen are 59.9 %, 17.2 %, 22.9 % respectively and their corresponding atomic percentage are 65.2 %, 16.1 %, 18.7 %. The presence of these elements was also further verified from FTIR spectroscopy.

The X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) analysis have been done for understanding the chemical composition of synthesized UCQDs. In Fig. 4 the XPS full spectra showed three peaks: C1s, N1s and O1s which indicated the presence of only three elements: carbon, nitrogen and oxygen. The C 1s analysis showed the presence of sp2/sp3 carbons (C–C/C=C) at 281.7 eV, C–O/C–N at 283.4 eV, and C=O at 285.8 eV. The N 1s band was deconvoluted into two peaks corresponding to C–N and N–H groups at 396.7 and 398.7 eV respectively [57]. The O 1s spectrum was also deconvoluted into two peaks corresponding to oxygen bonds of C–O/C=O and O–H species at 528.9 and 530 eV respectively [64]. Based on the elemental composition (C, N, O) obtained from EDX analysis and the bonding information between these elements obtained from XPS analysis, we proceeded to determine the most probable structure of the synthesized UCQDs using DFT simulation.

The energy difference (Δε) between highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) have shown a decreasing tendency with increasing the number of rings in the graphene core systems of predicted (UCQD)n structures (Table 2). Computed Δε (3.59 eV) of UCQD8 validates the Δε of synthesized UCQD (Figs. 2 and 8 ). Particle size (∼6.2 nm) estimated from simulated data of UCQD8 also validated by the synthesized UCQD size obtained from FESEM and experimental absorption data (Table 1, SD6).

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–vis emission analysis of synthesized UCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Synthesized UCQDs show variable luminescent properties under different excitation wavelengths. Under white light they appeared as brown whereas under UV radiation they appeared as blue (Scheme 1). Blueness in appearance confirms the possibility of presence of amino functional (≥NH2) groups over the surface of synthesized UCQDs [76]. Photoluminescent properties of synthesized UCQDs have been checked with their variable concentrations C1, C2 and C3 (Fig. 10 ). A strong emission peak at ∼460 nm was observed for lowest concentration of C1. With the increase in concentration up to C2, UCQDs showed dual emissions with strong peaks at ∼460 nm and ∼530 nm. With further increasing concentration the emission intensities of both peaks have been observed to be decreased and at concentration C3, only single less intense peak was observed at 530 nm. Maximum fluorescence intensity was observed at C2 concentration. Lesser photoluminescence at lowest concentration (C1) may be due to presence of less number of UCQDs in solution with same or similar surface morphology and passivation whereas at maximum concentration (C3), the less intensity may appear due to the presence of larger size of carbon particles (20–40 nm average diameter) or bulk phase formation with the same passivation as suggested by FESEM analysis (Fig. 3). We have chosen C2 for rest of the study. The variation of luminescent properties of C2 was investigated under variable excitation wavelength (290 nm< λ ex < 450 nm) in Fig. 11 . UCQD has shown unique dual fluorescence peaks first at 460 nm (for which the stronger signal recorded for λ ex = 400 nm) and another at 530 nm (the stronger signal recorded for λ ex = 435 nm) respectively. It is observed that the fluorescence peak intensity at 460 nm increases rapidly as the λ ex increases from 290 nm up to 400 nm. With further increase of λ ex from 405 nm to 440 nm, the fluorescence peak at 460 nm drops rapidly with the sharp increment of fluorescence peak at 530 nm with the presence of a clear iso-emissive point at ∼497 nm. The unaltered fluorescence peak positions with variable excitation wavelengths and appearance of clear iso-emissive point justifies the size independent behavior of synthesized UCQDs [77]. Usually most of the available CQDs and their complexes used to show red shift in their photoluminescence peak with the increase of λ ex but for the present case we did not observe any such changes. Appearance of two excitation independent emission peaks at 460 nm and 530 nm has revealed the existence of two preferable excited state species. The excitation independent behavior of emissive species also justifies the dominating presence of organic fluorophores than carbogenic cores in synthesized UCQDs [25]. The photoluminescence of the synthesized UCQDs were observed to be stable in terms of photoirradiation exhibiting no meaningful reduction in the observed intensities throughout the repeating excitations for several days (up to 3 months). Excitation spectra correspond to two emission peaks justify the absorption spectrum (Fig. 12 ).

Widely accepted mechanism of luminescent signal of nanomaterials is the radiative recombination of excitons. For CQDs, mechanistically the luminescence usually appears due to stabilization of their molecular structure by the presence of surface traps that become emissive upon surface passivation. Because of the large surface-to-volume ratio of CQDs due to surface passivation, there exists a quantum confinement of emissive energy traps to the particle surface, resulting in intense photoluminescence. For present synthesized UCQDs, due to the presence of surface traps created by amino groups, QD structures appeared as uniform and stable and have shown excitation independent strong photoluminescence characteristics. The enhanced fluorescence intensity may be due to the further surface modification or passivation by carboxylic and amino groups that stabilized the surface energy traps of the carbogenic nanoparticles and made them good emissive. Also, –NH2 is present on the surface of CQDs, the electrons of nitrogen in –NH2 increases the electron density and therefore decreases the band gap of surface states, resulting in emission peak in longer wavelength region (Scheme 2 ). Therefore, the possible charge transfer from nitrogen of amino groups to carbon core of these carbogenic nanoparticle may be another reason of the strong luminescence of UCQDs.

The computed emission spectrum obtained from UCQD8 structure (referred from section 3.3) shows a strong emission at 483.88 nm with oscillator strength of 0.209 for S1→S0 transition matches perfectly with the experimental emission corresponding to λ ex = 400 nm (Fig. 13 ). Table 4 shows the comparison between different parameters of UCQD8 cluster and synthesized UCQDs.

For preciously understanding the existence of emissive species for the synthesized UCQDs with concentration C2, fluorescence lifetime decay profiles have been studied. Two lifetime decay profile for synthesized UCQDs have been observed (Fig. 14 ). The obtained lifetime (τ) data of UCQDs were fitted by tri-exponential function as follows in equation no 3:

The presence of two decay curves with two average lifetimes leads to the confirmation of existence of two emissive species with two decays one from the singled layer graphene core state and another from the passivated state of synthesized UCQDs.

In the present study, urea-based carbon quantum dots (UCQDs) have been successfully synthesized from urea and citric acid combination via hydrothermal method. Structural, electronic and optical characterization of synthesized UCQDs were performed by using FESEM, XPS, UV–Vis absorption, emission, FTIR spectroscopic techniques and lifetime analysis. Variable concentration dependent particle sizes of prepared UCQDs were measured within the range from 4 nm to 40 nm. The particle size was found to be directly proportional to the concentration of UCQDs, whereas the band gap was found to be inversely proportional to the concentrations of UCQDs. At lower concentrations of UCQDs (C1, C2), surface passivation of the graphite cores were created by amino and carboxylic groups. The surface passivation also appeared through trapping the surface of CQDs by removing surface dangling bonds (O=C–H, C=O, C–O–C) which create two emissive species at excited state (S1). A quantum confinement of the energy bands due to surface trapped UCQDs exhibit strong dual photoluminescence at 460 nm and 530 nm from two preferable excited state species. The unaltered fluorescence peak positions with variable excitation wavelengths and appearance of clear iso-emissive point justifies the size independent behavior of synthesized UCQDs. The presence of two decay curves with two average lifetimes leads to the existence of two emissive species with two decays one from the singled layer graphene core state and another from the passivated state of synthesized UCQDs. At higher concentration (C3) due to bulk phase formation less fluorescence was observed which again validated the FESEM result. XPS analysis confirmed the presence of three elements carbon, nitrogen and oxygen. Individual spectra of C1s, N1s and O1s showed the presence of the various bonds (C–C, C=C, C–O, C–N, C=O, N–H and O–H) in synthesized UCQDs. Based on the elemental composition (C, N, O) obtained from EDX analysis and the bonding information between C, N, O obtained from XPS analysis, most probable structure of the synthesized UCQDs has been predicted which was further verified by computational analysis by using DFT. Among all possible guess structures, simulated data of (UCQD)8 has shown close resemblance with the Synthesized one. To confirm the structural feature further, observed optical band gap (∼3 eV) from UV data of the synthesized UCQDs has shown the close resemblance with (UCQD)8 having band gap of ∼3.59 eV. The best choice of computed UCQD structure has also been selected on the basis of similarity between other experimental and computational results (Δε, dipole moment, vibrational modes of νOH, νNH, νCH, νCO, δOH etc, absorption and emission wavelengths). A perfect match between theoretical FTIR, UV–Vis absorption and luminescence spectra of UCQD8 with synthesized one confirms the presence of carboxylic and amino surface trapping groups on the surface of graphene core which ensure the better stability and photoluminescent behavior prepared UCQDs. The observed spectral data has been also confirmed from FESEM and EDX spectra which further validate the presence of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen in synthesized UCQDs. As a youngest member of the carbon nanostructure family, knowledge about CQDs is growing and their applicability as a biocompatible sensor/detector or carrier is being intensively studied nowadays. We believe that the structural and spectral insight provided by our simulations, as well as the close correlation between experimental and computed data, will shed light on some of the fascinating phenomena in the novel field of CQD studies. Because of their extremely luminous nature and strong biocompatibility, UCQDs presented in current work will certainly be considered for use as fluorescent devices in the future.

Shruti Sharma: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Papia Chowdhury: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization.
